fact: July 4 falls exactly 185 days into the year, making it almost perfectly halfway through! That means there are exactly 180 days left until the end of the year.
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak summer mosquito season - female mosquitoes are at their hungriest and most annoying right around now!
The sun rises super early on July 4 in places like Alaska - some spots get sunrise at 4:30 AM and don't see sunset until almost 11 PM!
Cherry trees in the Southern Hemisphere are blooming with beautiful pink flowers right now, since it's the middle of winter there.
This date falls smack in the middle of Cancer season in the zodiac, which runs from June 21 to July 22.
Fireflies are at their flashiest peak in North America during early July - they blink the most on warm, humid nights like these!
The Earth is actually at its farthest point from the sun around July 4 each year, called aphelion - we're about 3 million miles farther away than in January!
In Australia and New Zealand, July 4 is deep winter, and some places might even have snow on the ground while North Americans are sweating!
Dandelions have finished blooming by now in most places and turned into those fluffy white seed balls that kids love to blow apart.
Ocean water temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are finally getting warm enough for comfortable swimming - the water has been soaking up summer heat for weeks!
Baby birds that hatched in spring are now teenagers learning to fly, so you might see lots of clumsy young birds crash-landing in yards around this time.
The Big Dipper constellation appears highest in the evening sky during early July, making it the perfect time to spot it right after sunset.
